perturbation.original_text: (vi) “Personal Information” means any information about an identifiable i ndividual as defined in the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada) and include s personal health information as defined in the Personal Health Info rmation Protection Act, 2004 (Ontar io), and any amending or successor legislation to either statute and th e regulations made pursuant thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: (vi) “Personal Information” means any information about an identifiable individual as defined in the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada), but excludes personal health information as defined in the Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004 (Ontario), and any amending or successor legislation to either statute and the regulations made pursuant thereto.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding personal health information from the definition of 'Personal Information', the contract contradicts Ontario's Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA) which explicitly includes it.
perturbation.law_citation: PHIPA, 2004, S.O. 2004, c. 3, Schedule A, s. 4(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: PHIPA defines 'personal health information' broadly. This exclusion contradicts PHIPA, potentially leading to non-compliance in handling health information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly excludes 'personal health information' from the definition of 'Personal Information'. This contradicts the purpose and scope of Ontario's Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA), as the Perturbation Explanation notes, which inherently includes such information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004, indicating its relevance and scope regarding health information. The exclusion directly undermines the protection intended by PHIPA.

perturbation.original_text: (vii) “Privacy Laws” means the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada), the Personal Health Information Protection Act , 2004 (Ontario) and any other laws enforceable in Ontario, now existing or which may be enacted in the future, which govern the collection, use, processing and/or disclosure - 2 - PRACTICE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM INTEGRATION TOOLKIT NON-DISCLOSURE AND PERMITTED USE AGREEMENT of personal information, and any ame nding or successor legislation and regul ations made pursuant thereto, ,all as may be amended from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: (vii) “Privacy Laws” means solely the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada), and excludes the Personal Health Information Protection Act , 2004 (Ontario) or any other provincial laws, regardless of whether they govern the collection, use, processing and/or disclosure of personal information.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding Ontario's PHIPA, the contract creates a conflict because PHIPA is a primary privacy law in Ontario.
perturbation.law_citation: PHIPA, 2004, S.O. 2004, c. 3, Schedule A, s. 3
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts Ontario's PHIPA by explicitly excluding it from the definition of 'Privacy Laws', making it unclear if PHIPA will be followed when handling personal information.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes Ontario's PHIPA from the definition of 'Privacy Laws'. This is a direct contradiction because PHIPA is a primary privacy law in Ontario. The perturbation explanation confirms that PHIPA is a primary privacy law in Ontario, and the law explanation confirms that the exclusion would create confusion about whether PHIPA will be followed when handling personal information. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (f) To the extent any Confidential Info rmation contains any Personal Informa tion, Vendor agrees to comply with applicable Privacy Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) To the extent any Confidential Information contains any Personal Information, Vendor agrees to comply with only the federal privacy laws and is explicitly not required to comply with provincial privacy laws such as Ontario's PHIPA.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the vendor only needs to comply with federal laws and not Ontario's PHIPA, the contract contradicts the legal requirements for handling personal health information in Ontario.
perturbation.law_citation: PHIPA, 2004, S.O. 2004, c. 3, Schedule A, s. 6
perturbation.law_explanation: This directly contradicts PHIPA, as it creates a loophole that allows the vendor to avoid responsibilities for protection of personal health information mandated by the law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Health Information Protection Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Ontario legislation established in 2004 Personal Health Information Protection Act Legislative Assembly of Ontario Long title An Ontario Act to establish consistent rules governing the collection, use and disclosure of personal health information in the hands of ’health information custodians‘, such as doctors, hospitals or other health care providers. Citation S.O. 2004, Chapter 3 Schedule A Enacted by Legislative Assembly of Ontario Assented to 20 May 2004 Commenced 1 November 2004 Legislative history Bill title Bill 31, Schedule A Introduced by Ministry of Consumer and Business Services and the Ministry of Health and Long Term Care The Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004 ( French : Loi de 2004 sur la protection des renseignements personnels sur la santé ), also known as PHIPA , is Ontario legislation established in November 2004. PHIPA is one of two components of the Health Information Protection Act 2004 . [ 1 ] The Health Information Protection Act , also established in 2004, comprises two schedules: PHIPA (Schedule A) and the Quality of Care Information Protection Act (Schedule B). [ 1 ] The PHIPA replaced the Health Cards and Numbers Control Act (SO 1991, c 1). [ 2 ] PHIPA provides a set of rules for the collection, use and disclosure of personal health information by a "Health Information Custodian" (HIC), and includes the following provisions: [ 1 ] Consent is required for the collection, use and disclosure of personal health information, with few exceptions HICs are required to treat all personal health information as confidential and maintain its security Individuals have a right to access their personal health information, as well as the right to correct errors Individuals have the right to instruct HICs not to share their personal health information with others Rules are provided for the use of personal health information for fundraising or marketing purposes Guidelines are set for the use and disclosure of personal health information as a secondary use such as research, quality
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA) by explicitly stating that the vendor is not required to comply with provincial privacy laws like Ontario's PHIPA. The original text mandates compliance with 'applicable Privacy Laws,' but the changed text carves out an exception for provincial laws, specifically PHIPA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004, S.O. 2004, c. 3, Sched. A, establishing its legal basis. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that PHIPA provides a set of rules for the collection, use and disclosure of personal health information by a 'Health Information Custodian,' which the changed text seeks to circumvent. Therefore, the modification results in a clear non-compliance with a core legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: (e) If either party employs lawyers to en force any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable lawyers’ fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, and the parties further cons ent to non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal or provincial courts sitting in Toronto, Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) If either party employs lawyers to en force any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover lawyers’ fees and costs only if a judge deems those fees and costs to be 'extraordinary'. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, and the parties further cons ent to non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal or provincial courts sitting in Toronto, Ontario.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'only if a judge deems those fees and costs to be 'extraordinary'', the contract introduces ambiguity and contradicts the common understanding of 'reasonable lawyers' fees and costs' under Ontario law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 57.01 allows for recovery of reasonable legal fees. By adding the term 'extraordinary,' the contract is more restrictive than typical interpretations of this rule, increasing the burden for fee recovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Rule 57. Declaratory Judgment | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 57. Declaratory Judgment These rules govern the procedure for obtaining a declaratory judgment under 28 U.S.C. §2201 . Rules 38 and 39 govern a demand for a jury trial. The existence of another adequate remedy does not preclude a declaratory judgment that is otherwise appropriate. The court may order a speedy hearing of a declaratory-judgment action. Notes (As amended Dec. 29, 1948, eff. Oct. 20, 1949; Apr. 30, 2007, eff. Dec. 1, 2007.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Rules—1937 The fact that a declaratory judgment may be granted “whether or not further relief is or could be prayed” indicates that declaratory relief is alternative or cumulative and not exclusive or extraordinary. A declaratory judgment is appropriate when it will “terminate the controversy” giving rise to the proceeding. Inasmuch as it often involves only an issue of law on undisputed or relatively undisputed facts, it operates frequently as a summary proceeding, justifying docketing the case for early hearing as on a motion, as provided for in California (Code Civ.Proc. (Deering, 1937) §1062a), Michigan (3 Comp.Laws (1929) §13904), and Kentucky (Codes (Carroll, 1932) Civ.Pract. §639a–3). The “controversy” must necessarily be “of a justiciable nature, thus excluding an advisory decree upon a hypothetical state of facts.” Ashwander v. Tennessee Valley Authority , 297 U.S. 288, 325, 56 S.Ct. 466, 473, 80 L.Ed. 688, 699 (1936). The existence or nonexistence of any right, duty, power, liability, privilege, disability, or immunity or of any fact upon which such legal relations depend, or of a status, may be declared. The petitioner must have a practical interest in the declaration sought and all parties having an interest therein or adversely affected must be made parties or be cited. A declaration may not be rendered if a special statutory proceeding has been provided for the adjudication of some special type of case, but general ordinary or extraordinary legal remedies, whether regulated by statute or not, are not deemed special statutory proceedings. When declaratory relief will not be effective in settling the controversy, the court may decline to grant it. But the fact that another remedy would be equally effective affords no ground for declining declaratory relief. The demand for relief shall state with precision the declaratory judgment desired, to which may be joined a demand for coercive relief, cumulatively or in the alternative; but when coercive relief only is sought but is deemed ungrantable or inappropriate, the court may sua sponte , if it serves a useful purpose, grant instead a declaration of rights. Hasselbring v. Koepke , 263 Mich. 466, 248 N.W. 869, 93 A.L.R. 1170 (1933). Written instruments, including ordinances and statutes, may be construed before or after breach at the petition of a properly interested party, process being served on the private parties or public officials interested. In other respects the Uniform Declaratory Judgment Act affords a guide to the scope and function of the Federal act. Compare Aetna Life Insurance Co. v. Haworth , 300 U.S. 227, 57 S.Ct. 461 (1937); Nashville, Chattanooga & St. Louis Ry. v. Wallace , 288 U.S. 249 (1933); Gully, Tax Collector v. Interstate Natural Gas Co ., 82 F.(2d) 145 (C.C.A.5th, 1936); Ohio Casualty Ins. Co. v. Plummer , 13 F.Supp. 169 (S.D.Tex., 1935); Borchard, Declaratory Judgments (1934), passim. Notes of Advisory Committee on Rules—1948 Amendment The amendment substitutes the present statutory reference. Committee Notes on Rules—2007 Amendment The language of Rule 57 has been amended as part of the general restyling of the Civil Rules to make them more easily understood and to make style and terminology consistent throughout the rules. These changes are intended to be stylistic only. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Toolbox Wex:
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the prevailing party to recover 'reasonable lawyers’ fees and costs'. The changed text alters this by adding 'only if a judge deems those fees and costs to be 'extraordinary''. This change introduces a condition that deviates from the standard understanding of 'reasonable lawyers’ fees and costs'. According to the Law Explanation, this change is more restrictive than typical interpretations, increasing the burden for fee recovery. While the scraped snippets do not directly address the definition of reasonable fees, the alteration introduces ambiguity that can make the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (c) IBC has or expects to enter into service agreements dire ctly with Providers that will govern such Provider’s access to and use of the HCAI System. However, IBC does not guarantee availability or ope ration of the HCAI System to Vendor. Without prejudice to any right an individual Provider may have against IBC, IBC shall have no liability to Vendor in respect of any failure or problem in the operation of the HCAI System, including any communication proble ms between Providers and the HCAI System. IN NO EVENT WILL THE MEASURE OF DAMAGES PAYABLE BY EITHER PARTY INCLUDE, NOR WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR, ANY CONSEQUE NTIAL, INDIRECT, INCI DENTAL, EXEMPLARY OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATI ON, DAMAGES DUE TO BUSINESS INTERRUPTION OR LOST PROFITS, SAVINGS, COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE OR GOODWILL) ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/ OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM, REGARDLESS OF THE TYPE OF CLAIM, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING FUNDAMENTAL BREACH OR FAILURE OF THE ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY REMEDY CONTAINED HEREIN), NEGLIGENCE, STRICT LIABILIT Y OR OTHER LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY, WHETHER OR NOT FORESEEABLE, AND REGARDLESS OF THE CAUSE OF SUCH DAMAGES EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES IN ADVANCE. IBC’S LIABILITY FOR ANY MATTER RELATING TO OR ARISING FROM THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM, WHETHER BASED UPON AN ACTION OR CLAIM IN CONTRACT, WARRANTY, EQUITY, TORT, NEGLIGEN CE, OR OTHERWISE, SHALL NOT EXCEED, IN THE AGGREGATE AND IN RESPECT OF ALL ACTIONS, CLAIMS AND DAMAGES , AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO FIVE HUNDRED DOLLARS.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) IBC has or expects to enter into service agreements dire ctly with Providers that will govern such Provider’s access to and use of the HCAI System. However, IBC does not guarantee availability or ope ration of the HCAI System to Vendor. Without prejudice to any right an individual Provider may have against IBC, IBC shall have no liability to Vendor in respect of any failure or problem in the operation of the HCAI System, including any communication proble ms between Providers and the HCAI System. IBC’S LIABILITY FOR ANY MATTER RELATING TO OR ARISING FROM THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM IS CAPPED AT ONE DOLLAR, REGARDLESS OF THE CAUSE.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the liability cap to $1 could be deemed unconscionable, especially if the vendor incurs substantial damages due to IBC's actions or inactions, contradicting principles of contract law regarding fair and reasonable limitations of liability.
perturbation.law_citation: Tercon Contractors Ltd. v. British Columbia (Transportation and Highways), 2010 SCC 4
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating a specific statute, limiting liability to $1 could be deemed unconscionable under common law principles, especially as articulated in cases like Tercon, if it renders the contract fundamentally unfair and defeats the purpose of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modification drastically reduces IBC's potential liability to a nominal amount ($1), which, as the Perturbation Explanation suggests, could be deemed unconscionable if the vendor incurs substantial damages. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is irrelevant and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, the Law Explanation indicates that this limitation could be considered unfair under common law principles, potentially defeating the purpose of the agreement. The Misaligned Terminology definition, states that the legal contradiction arises when the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. Here, the liability cap is so low, that it is essentially unenforceable.
